original_abstract: Text detection in natural scene images is an important prerequisite for many content-based image analysis tasks. In this paper, we propose an accurate and robust method for detecting texts in natural scene images. A fast and effective pruning algorithm is designed to extract Maximally Stable Extremal Regions (MSERs) as character candidates using the strategy of minimizing regularized variations. Character candidates are grouped into text candidates by the single-link clustering algorithm, where distance weights and clustering threshold are learned automatically by a novel self-training distance metric learning algorithm. The posterior probabilities of text candidates corresponding to non-text are estimated with a character classifier; text candidates with high non-text probabilities are eliminated and texts are identified with a text classifier. The proposed system is evaluated on the ICDAR 2011 Robust Reading Competition database; the f-measure is over 76%, much better than the state-of-the-art performance of 71%. Experiments on multilingual, street view, multi-orientation and even born-digital databases also demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.
humanized_abstract: Text detection in natural scene images is often considered as a fundamental step for many content-based image analysis tasks. In this paper, an accurate and robust approach to detecting texts in natural scene images is proposed. Firstly, a fast pruning method that follows the principle of minimizing the regularized variations is developed to locate characters using the concept of MSER (Maximally Stable Extremal Regions). Candidate characters (RCs) are then segmented into potential text regions using the single-link clustering algorithm, in which the related distance weights and clustering threshold are both automatically learned by developing a self-training distance metric learning approach. After classifying the non-text likelihoods of the potential text regions (TRs) by a classifier on characters, all the regions with large non-text likelihood values are discarded and the remaining ones are detected as the final text regions using a text classifier. Experiments on the ICDAR 2011 competition test dataset demonstrate that the final detection performance of our system is of f-measure 76.10% (76.09% precision and 76.11% recall), which outperforms all the competition teams and existing literature by more than 5% and 5% (or even more than 5% more than the best result for certain literature), respectively. The results on various diverse datasets such as multilingual scenes, street view scenes, multi-orientation scenes and even some images including born-digital texts all support the efficiency of our approach.
